item.title: micro notes
2026-01-20 viruses are people too

item.description: <p>(original to <a href="https://www.facebook.com/tbyfield/posts/pfbid032KiBobukb5EiW2LFafin89gn4XMFYVxNS4aNZxahXUMR9h7tQ262JiUy847RX2cBl" class="external">FB<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a>)</p> <p>Trump &#x26; Co’s theory was that if they made an example of the Twin Cities, the rest of the country would cower in fear, but that isn’t working out at all. They can’t do this to the whole country, or even to more than a few mid-sized cities at a time. And with each repetition, the country learns more about these pathetic fascists and how to fight them. There aren’t many more escalatory steps Trump &#x26; Co can take before they open fire on an unarmed crowd, and I think they’ll do that soon. They probably hope that will be the opening gun of a civil war, but instead it’ll decisively mark the end of Trumpism — and the beginning of bringing this scum to justice.</p>

item.title: 2026-01-16-lowering the bar in FL etc
2026-01-08 Trump and residential rackets
2025-12-31 obey in advance
2025-12-29 on chomsky and epstein
2025-09-21 where do you draw the line
item.description: <p>[orig <a href="https://www.facebook.com/tbyfield/posts/pfbid02i2V3TeB9ew7HReNpDKLZ7TaFZ46yvMP7d4kpe9Uo43rurCcj2t1EagijfZ3bi1MNl" class="external">to FB<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a>]</p> <p>Everyone seems surprised that a handful of mega corps are vulnerable to political control, as if the left hadn’t been warning about that for decades. But rather than look backward, let’s look forward and ask what <em>is</em> resistant to political control? If it isn’t journalism, big tech, law firms, universities, or media, what is it? Easy: people. So rather than ritually reenacting this faux betrayal each time some sector shows its true colors, try asking yourself questions like: Where do you draw the line? What are you willing to risk? What will you demand from “opposition” politicians? How will you protect everyone who depends on you?</p>

item.title: recent things
2025-10-11 TrumpRX
2025 10 07 no more acab thanks
item.description: <p><img src="../../blg/new/new_images/2025-10-07-not-an-ice-protest.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed | center"></p> <p>A friend shared this image on FB, with the caption “Portland Protestors Baiting ICE Agents. With Donuts on Fishing Poles. Calling it ICE Fishing.” But rather than hector an lecture friends and allies in comments, I’m going to start sharing things I’m more iffy about. This is an old photo, maybe from Portland, but this definitely isn’t ICE. I don’t say that just to be pedantic, though. As blue-vs-green conflicts become more common — and they <em>will</em> — one side will always be worse than the other, but which one will be unpredictable and sometimes fluid. In this context, it’ll be a really good idea to lose the “ACAB”-style logic / rhetoric / imagery. And, more generally, my experience has always been that, in complicated conflicts, the people who are first to espouse hard-line, reductive morals are the last ones you should trust. It’s the people who always see things in shades of gray are the ones with abiding principles. But gray is metaphor; now it’ll be shades of blue and green.</p>

item.title: 2002 > The Blur Banff Project
2025-08-25 what isn't happening
image stream
2025-08-11-titles I through X
2025-08-11 learning to read corruption
neoligistical things
2025-05-31 science is what scientists do
item.description: <p><img src="../../blg/new/new_images/2025-05-31-gleick-chart.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p> <p>This list could be longer: China (esp. the Cultural revolution), Cambodia, and the massive purges across civil society and education in Turkey after <a href="../../blg/old/social-media/2020/2020-12-19-on-the-turkish-pseudo-coup-and-zeynep-tufekci" class="internal alias" data-slug="blg/old/social-media/2020/2020-12-19-on-the-turkish-pseudo-coup-and-zeynep-tufekci">that fake attempted “coup” several years ago</a> — off the top of my head. Gleick’s point may be true (I think it is, of course), but it also combines the directionaless retrospection of the Sanatayana clichés with what I’ve started criticizing as “modelism.” Great, we have models — so what? What should be happening is the directly affected communities — scientists and adjacent workers involved in coordination, funding, publishing, supporting industries, etc, should be actively planning how to rebuild even better institutions. There’s no shortage of models for that, large and small: generation upon generation of scientific and techniocal societies, WW1-era standardization, WW2-era massive coordination (aviation, radar, optics, aerospace medicine are the ones I know a lot about), legends like Lockheed Martin’s skunkworks, the transnational scientific networks that enabled fields from meteorology to epidemiology, innovative entities like IAVI, ACT-UP and TAG, private-ish entities like Princeton’s IAS — the list goes on and on. Yes, Trump is doing incalculable damage, but the point of Sanatayana’s injunction shouldn’t be hand-wringing, it should be choosing the right lessons to learn from and then applying that learning. The scientific comunity is <em>the</em> longue-durée model for actively building rational, deliberate social and institutional structures with little or no regard for the comings and goings of politics. Now’s the time to shine.</p>

item.title: 2025-05-27 a bleak prospect
item.description: <p>Catalist’s <a href="https://catalist.us/whathappened2024/" class="external">“What Happened in 2024?”<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> is a bleak, bleak post mortem of the 2024 election. Blame the electorate’s racism, blame its sexism, blame its ageism, blame whatever you want — then ask yourself whether that factor will be less of a problem before the next elections. (No.) Diagnose the problem as this bumbling, or that delusional strategy, or some other disorganizational problem — then ask yourself whether that factor will be less of a problem before the next elections. (No.) Chalk it up to “genocide,” if you like. Is the current national Democratic Party moving on that issue? No. Are they doing what they need to before the next elections. (No.) Are there signs of aggressive, relevant new generations of leadership coming up? (No.) Do monthly one-day national protests contribute to solving these problems? (No.) I’m <em>very</em> tempted to state the problem this way: <em>The Democratic Party is the #1 obstacle to progress in this country, and any argument or strategy that relies on them is doomed to fail.</em> I’m not quite there yet, but not far off.</p>

item.title: 2025-05-27 biden and student debt
2025-05-23 genocide, genocides, and Trump
2025-05-22 the silence of the lames
2025-05-20 a modest book proposal
item.description: <p><img src="../../blg/new/new_images/chicago-fake-books.jpeg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p> <p>🎩 Rick Perlstein for this image. It’s a pity that we’re at a point, historically speaking, when — justified or not — we view everything through the lens of tragedy rather than comedy. (It seems to me that the idea of “queering” points us toward the exit from that, but I digress.) AI’s tendency to exaggerate, to get things right-sounding but wrong in fact, to generate statistical grotesques on every level — these are all the hallmarks not of tragedy but of comedy: parody, irony, and cartoonishness to the point of slapststickery. It’s basically a <em>stochastic comedy of manners</em>. And in that view, the idea of a book review inventing books to review is brilliant and damning to a degree that would give Swift a run for his money.</p>

item.title: 2025-04-01 DOGE and the wages of sin
2025-04-01 edus can’t buy their way out of fascism
2025-03-25 DOD says signal is a no-no
item.description: <p><img src="../../blg/new/new_images/2025-03-25-signal-opsec-email.jpeg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relaytive|center"></p> <p>The plot thickens. <a href="https://www.npr.org/2025/03/25/nx-s1-5339801/pentagon-email-signal-vulnerability" class="external">NPR reports<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> this image was taken from a Pentagon-wide email circulated on 18 March. The headline says “Ahead of the Signal leak,” which is true and not: Goldberg was added on the 11th, the thread took place on the 13th, and the attacks were on the 15th — so this warning came three days later than the events, but six days before Goldberg’s story ran. That means it came <em>after</em> he started asking questions, which to my ear suggests this may well have been DoD bureaucracy (a) doing the right thing opsec-wise AND ALSO — because this kind of signaling and maneuvering is how bureaucracies work — moving to undermine the people involved in the chat.</p>

item.title: 2025-03-25 vice signaling
item.description: <p>The dynamics around this Signal / <em>Atlantic</em> leak are interesting. It was obvious from the start that Trump’s people were using Signal in order to circumvent federal record-keeping requirements, but only hard-core procedural nerds on social media noted that. Everyone else got sucked into mediated pearl-clutching over the opsec aspect. Lib / prog / left types got sucked into that framing too, rather than focus on the underlying issues that — you’d think — should be consistent with their rhetoric: opposition to militarism, to US as globocop, to arbitrary attacks, to targeting individuals, etc. And, at a time when the very idea of Congressional oversight is under ideological attack as such, the Senate sprang into action <em>immediately</em> and is holding hearings? I really get the sense that no one believes anything, and everyone’s stuck on some endless political cosplay treadmill.</p>

item.title: 2025-03-25 Trump and “nuclear codes” — round 2

2025-03-21 alma mater
item.description: <p>Via <a href="https://bsky.app/profile/mlobelart.bsky.social" class="external">Michael Lobel<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> on Bsky, Daniel Chester French’s plaster maquette for his “Alma Mater” sculpture at Columbia (<a href="https://bsky.app/profile/mlobelart.bsky.social/post/3lkw6aji75k2g" class="external">link<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a>):</p> <p><img src="../../blg/new/new_images/2025-03-21-alma-mater-maquette.jpg" width="auto" height="auto" alt="wmed relative|center"></p>

item.title: 2025-03-13 this is not an an attack on science

image stream 3
software
2019-12-23 reviewing cats as bloodsport
item.description: <p>Welcome to Issue #1 of R4, my periodic review of reviews of review reviews. So, like, people write reviews of something, then other people review them, then someone collects <em>them</em> — and I review those collections! Got it? Good. Issue #1 is dedicated to the Digg’s collection of “The Funniest, Meanest Lines From The Very Bad Reviews For The ‘Cats’ Movie.” REVIEW: The last few days have been a Garden of Earthly Delights when it comes to hilarious, imaginative things to say about CATS. Somehow, Digg succeeded in the statistically impossible task of collecting lines that are emphatically neither. RATING: 💩 —— (1 out of 5). The only reason Digg gets that 1 is so you’ll know the rating system uses poops. If there were a way to do a fecal transplant and <em>insert</em> poops back into Digg so it could get a negative-poop rating, they’d deserve it for this (wait for it…) piece of sh*t.</p>

item.title: 2020-04-22 competitive health care, sorta
item.description: <p><a href="https://www.washingtonpost.com/nation/2020/04/22/las-vegas-coronavirus-reopen/" class="external">The mayor of Las Vegas:<svg aria-hidden="true" class="external-icon" style="max-width:0.8em;max-height:0.8em" viewBox="0 0 512 512"><path d="M320 0H288V64h32 82.7L201.4 265.4 178.7 288 224 333.3l22.6-22.6L448 109.3V192v32h64V192 32 0H480 320zM32 32H0V64 480v32H32 456h32V480 352 320H424v32 96H64V96h96 32V32H160 32z"></path></svg></a> “Assume everybody is a carrier. And then you start from an even slate. And tell the people what to do. And let the businesses open and competition will destroy that business if, in fact, they become evident that they have disease, they’re closed down. It’s that simple.” Or maybe, rather than assuming that businesses are the basic unit of social analysis, we could assume <em>cities</em> are — and when it becomes evident that Las Vegas has the disease, it gets closed down. It’s that simple. 👍🏼</p>
